title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers

input_row.title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
input_row.description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
input_row.rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context. Impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
input_row.audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
input_row.remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers. Default Value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/security-context/ 2. https://learn.cisecurity.org/benchmarks
input_row.impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_pod_and_container_security_context_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- Enumerate all Pod resources in all namespaces using the Kubernetes API (e.g., via kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json or equivalent client SDK calls).
- For each Pod, inspect the spec.securityContext field at the Pod level.
- For each container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) within each Pod, inspect the securityContext field at the container level.
- Validate that at least one securityContext property is set at either the Pod or container level. Key fields to check for presence include (but are not limited to): runAsUser, runAsGroup, fsGroup, seLinuxOptions, capabilities, allowPrivilegeEscalation, privileged, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile, etc.
- Pass Criteria: Every Pod and every container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) must have a non-null securityContext field defined with at least one security-relevant property set.
- Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) that lacks a securityContext field or has an empty securityContext object (i.e., no security-relevant properties set).
- Example CLI command for manual spot-check:
  ```
  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {pod: .metadata.name, namespace: .metadata.namespace, podSecurityContext: .spec.securityContext, containerSecurityContexts: [.spec.containers[].securityContext]}'
  ```
- The automation should report all non-compliant Pods/containers, including their namespace and name, and specify which securityContext fields are missing.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.7.1 PDF
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The task involves enumerating Pods and their containers across all namespaces and checking their securityContext fields, which is efficiently done using automation.
- This can indeed be checked via Kubernetes APIs, kubectl, and CLI commands. The use of `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` is appropriate for gathering the necessary data.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically correct. The method of enumerating Pods and checking the securityContext fields at both the Pod and container levels is sound.
- The kubectl command provided for manual spot-checking is valid and correctly uses `jq` to parse JSON output, which is a common practice.
- The program name `k8s_pod_and_container_security_context_configured` is descriptive but could be more specific regarding what is being checked. It does follow a reasonable convention but lacks specificity about the security context properties being validated.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved for clarity and specificity. A suggestion would be `k8s_pod_container_security_context_presence_check` to indicate that the check is specifically for the presence of security context configurations.
- Consideration for more detailed reporting on which specific securityContext fields are missing could enhance the automation. This would provide more actionable insights for remediation.
- While the current approach is effective, exploring Kubernetes Custom Resources or using tools like Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Gatekeeper could offer more flexibility and integration into CI/CD pipelines for continuous compliance checks.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured and aligns with Kubernetes security best practices. The use of Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands is appropriate and effective for the task.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name to `k8s_pod_container_security_context_presence_check` for improved clarity and specificity.
- Consider enhancing the automation to provide detailed feedback on which securityContext fields are absent, aiding in targeted remediation efforts.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach should be automated, as the task of enumerating Pods and checking their security contexts can be efficiently performed using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. The automation details provided are technically sound and align with the capabilities of Kubernetes, allowing for a comprehensive check of security contexts across all Pods and containers.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
k8s_pod_container_security_context_presence_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods
- API/CLI Method: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json
- Properties to Check: spec.securityContext at both Pod and container levels, specifically looking for fields such as runAsUser, runAsGroup, fsGroup, capabilities, allowPrivilegeEscalation, privileged, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile, etc.
- Pass Criteria: Every Pod and every container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) must have a non-null securityContext field defined with at least one security-relevant property set.
- Fail Criteria: Any Pod or container (including initContainers and ephemeralContainers) that lacks a securityContext field or has an empty securityContext object (i.e., no security-relevant properties set).
